name: Maturity Model for Technical Documentation
description: Welcome to the Technical Documentation Maturity Model. The Technical Documentation Maturity Model is a questionnaire-based tool for assessing the maturity level of technical documentation. It was developed to assist companies in the sustainable improvement of their technical documentation.  The maturity model is based on the results of research into existing maturity models and the analysis of interviews. The maturity model consists of 33 subdimensions, divided into 8 dimensions, and should be understood as a framework. This means that the aspects of the dimensions can be interpreted differently depending on the organization. Users are encouraged to add their own company-specific items. Contact information for feedback and questions: christian4.koch@tu-dortmund.de The tool covers the following areas. The respective items under consideration are highlighted in color. Technical Documentation (TD): Relevant topics within technical documentation. Organization (OG): Relevant organizational topics that are related to or influence technical documentation.

glossary.API (Application Programming Interface): A component of a software system that is made available to other programs to connect to the system (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programmierschnittstelle)
glossary.CCMS (Component Content Management Syste: A specialised form of a content management system (CMS) that has been developed specifically for the management, storage and reuse of content in a granular form, known as components (https://carstens-techdok.de/ccms/)
glossary.CMS (Content Management System): Software or service for the collaborative creation, editing, organisation, presentation and delivery of digital content for use in websites, apps, digital signage and other media formats (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Content-Management-System)
glossary.Corporate Design: Part of corporate branding. It encompasses the visual and linguistic staging of the brand and serves to make the identity and values of a company visible (https://www.syndicate.de/was-ist-corporate-design/#:~:text=Das%20Corporate%20Design%20ist%20neben,eines%20Unternehmens%20sichtbar%20zu%20machen.)
glossary.Corporate Identity: The totality of characteristics that distinguish a company and set it apart from other companies. Corporate identity is therefore the company's self-image (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporate_Identity)
glossary.CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility): Integration of ecological, social and economic factors into company operations on a voluntary and sometimes also legal basis. The activities of the companies are understood here in the broadest sense: economic activities, internal interactions (employees, managers, shareholders) and external interactions (suppliers, customers) (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporate_Social_Responsibility)
glossary.DITA (Darwin Information Typing Architec: Document format similar to the well-known DocBook. DITA is developed by the OASIS DITA Technical Committee as a free architecture and is available as a document type definition (DTD) free of charge. The format is based on XML: unlike DocBook, there is no SGML-based document type definition (DTD) for DITA (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin_Information_Typing_Architecture)
glossary.ECLASS: Data standard for the classification of products and services using standardized ISO-compliant characteristics. The ECLASS standard enables the digital exchange of product master data across industries, countries, languages or organisations (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/ECLASS)
glossary.ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): Entrepreneurial task of planning, controlling and managing personnel, resources, capital, operating resources, material and information and communication technology in terms of the company's purpose in a timely manner and according to requirements. This is intended to ensure an efficient operational value creation process and a constantly optimised control of corporate and operational processes (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enterprise-Resource-Planning)
glossary.Ethernet: Technology that specifies software (protocols, etc.) and hardware (cables, hubs, network cards, etc.) for wired data networks, originally intended for local area networks (LANs) and therefore also known as LAN technology. It enables the exchange of data in the form of data frames between devices connected in a local network (LAN) (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethernet)
glossary.Fieldbus: Bus system that connects field devices such as sensors and actuators in a plant to communicate with an automation device. When several communication participants send their messages over the same line, it must be defined who (identifier) says what (measurement value, command) when (initiative). There are standardised protocols for this (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feldbus)
glossary.Headless Delivery: Refers to an architecture that decouples the content backend from the frontend display, so that content can be distributed via an API to any channels and end devices. In contrast to conventional systems, only the pure content (often as data such as JSON) is delivered to the user interface in headless delivery, which enables greater flexibility and scalability in the personalisation of content across different channels (https://documentation.coremedia.com/discover/api/headless-server-api/)
glossary.iiRDS (intelligent information Request a: Technical standard for the delivery of digital user information, such as electronic operating manuals and user instructions (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/IiRDS)
glossary.Middleware: Application-neutral programs that mediate between applications in such a way that the complexity of these applications and their infrastructure is concealed (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middleware)
glossary.Semantic model: Within the framework of data modelling, an abstract, formal description and representation of a section of the “perceived world” in a particular context (e.g. a project). Various modelling languages exist for the formulation of semantic data models, the most well-known of which is the entity-relationship model (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semantisches_Datenmodell)
glossary.Omnichannel: Cross-channel business model for companies to improve customer experience management. The approach is used in healthcare, government agencies and financial services as well as in the retail and telecommunications sectors, with channels such as physical locations, FAQ websites, social media, live web chats, mobile applications and telephone communication. Companies that implement the omnichannel concept argue that customers want to be in constant contact with a company via several channels at the same time (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omnichannel)
glossary.Ontology: Linguistically expressed and formally ordered representations of a set of terms and the relationships between them in a specific subject area (based on the classical concept of ontology). They are used to exchange “knowledge” in digitized and formal form between processes (often application programmes) and services. Knowledge includes both general knowledge and knowledge about very specific subject areas and processes (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ontologie_(Informatik))
glossary.OPC UA: Standard for data exchange as a platform-independent, service-oriented architecture (SOA)[1]. It introduces the ability not only to transport machine data (control variables, measured values, parameters, etc.) but also to describe it semantically in a machine-readable form (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/OPC_Unified_Architecture)
glossary.PDM (Product Data Management): Stores and manages product-defining, representing and presenting data as a result of product development and makes it available in downstream phases of the product lifecycle (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Produktdatenmanagement)
glossary.PIM (Product Information Management): Provision of product information for use in various output media or distribution channels as well as for different locations (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Produktinformationsmanagement)
glossary.PLM (Product Lifecycle Management): Concept for the seamless integration of all information that accrues during the life cycle of a product (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Product-Lifecycle-Management)
glossary.Cross-functional team: Groups of people with a variety of skills and experiences who all work towards a common goal. Instead of passing a project from team to team until the market launch date, a single team can work on every aspect of the project (https://www.dropbox.com/de/resources/cross-functional-teams)
glossary.Terminology mapping: Assignment of terms from different terminologies to reconcile or harmonise their meanings.
glossary.Translation Memory: Database with structured translations that represents the main component of computer-aided translation (CAT) applications (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%9Cbersetzungsspeicher)
glossary.URI (Uniform Resource Identifier): Identifier consisting of a character string that is used to identify an abstract or physical resource. URIs are used to designate resources (such as web pages, other files, invocation of web services, but also e-mail recipients) on the internet and especially in the World Wide Web (https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier)

dimensions.name: Editorial Process - Process Initiation
dimensions.description: Clearly define the starting point for editorial activities, initiate the necessary steps, and establish the required framework (e.g., responsibilities, resources, timeline) to ensure the smooth production of documentation.

process_profile.purpose: Clearly define the starting point for editorial work, initiate the necessary steps, and establish the required framework (e.g., responsibilities, resources, timeline) to ensure the smooth production of documentation.
process_profile.results:
• Resource planning and process initiation have been defined
• The schedule and milestone plan are available
• The editorial process is linked to the development process
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Identify and document the trigger for the editorial process
• Ensure that all necessary resources are available
• Link the activities of the editorial process with those of the development process
process_profile.work_products:
• Start documentation
• Project Schedule
• Resource Plan

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Employees have the practical knowledge to independently initiate the editorial process

questions.text: Is it ensured that employees know—based on their experience or established knowledge—when they need to begin the editorial process within the development cycle?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The overall resources required for the editorial process are roughly known (e.g., number of person-months)
• Employees have sufficient resources to carry out the editorial work
• Deadlines for completing the documentation have been set and are generally met
• Plans are in place to align the editorial process with the development process
• Guidelines exist for the tasks involved in the editorial process
levels.benefit:
• Earlier planning, as the initial criteria for launch are in place
• Avoidance of critical delays caused by the editorial team in individual cases
• Increased efficiency through initial clarification of responsibilities
--> Initial stability

questions.text: In development processes, are the general resources required for the editorial process known in advance? Are sufficient resources available?
Are deadlines for completing the documentation defined in development processes? Is it possible to identify a starting point for the editorial process?
Has a plan been established for setting up an editorial process aligned with the development process (for example, in a roadmap)?
Is there a guideline for the tasks to be performed during the editorial process, and is it updated periodically?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The editorial process has been broken down into individual work packages
• The work packages are linked to work packages in the development process
• The individual work packages are subject to specific resource planning
• Actual resource requirements are documented
• Actual and planned resource requirements are regularly compared
• Time-sensitive editorial tasks are identified, and appropriate buffers are factored in
• Areas for improvement in the editorial process are identified
levels.benefit:
• Clear starting points increase the reliability of processes
• Improved predictability of resources and deadlines
• Breaking tasks down into individual work packages increases transparency in the editorial process and makes it easier to identify opportunities for improvement
--> Reliability and efficiency

questions.text: Has the editorial process been broken down into work packages, and are these linked to specific development tasks?
Are there regular consultations with all relevant stakeholders?
Was resource planning carried out for individual tasks in the editorial process?
Are actual resource requirements documented and regularly compared with the planning data?
Are time-sensitive editorial tasks identified, and are they factored in with an appropriate buffer?
Are there opportunities for improvement in the editorial process?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Resource planning is system-supported and integrated into the development process
• Performance data from the editorial process is used for further analysis
• Workflows have been defined
• Tasks are automatically sent to the responsible parties with deadlines
levels.benefit:
• No delays (caused by the editorial team) in the market launch
• Improved collaboration between the editorial team and development
• A transparent flow of information reduces media breaks and errors
--> Time and quality benefits

questions.text: Is system-supported resource planning integrated into the development process?
Is the performance data from the editorial process (e.g., actual time requirements) used for other purposes (e.g., to improve forecasts of resource requirements)?
Have workflows been defined? Are tasks with deadlines sent to the responsible parties (e.g., via a ticket system)?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• When changes are made to the development process, the relevant tasks are triggered in the editorial process
• When changes are made to the development process, resources are dynamically adjusted
• Resources are automatically planned and prioritized
• Based on current developments, opportunities for improvement are identified, evaluated, and implemented
levels.benefit:
• Documentation becomes a strategic enabler, supports agile development processes, and helps capture knowledge early on
• Automated initiation reduces manual effort
• Eases the burden on the organization through proactive resource planning
--> strategic use and competitive advantage

questions.text: Is the editorial process triggered when changes are made to the development process (e.g., product changes)? Are resources dynamically adjusted?
Are the resources needed for the editorial process automatically planned and prioritized (e.g., based on project progress)?
Are opportunities for improvement in the editorial process identified, evaluated, and implemented based on current developments?

dimensions.name: Editorial Process - Information Gathering
dimensions.description: The process of gathering the information needed for the editorial process

process_profile.purpose: The process of gathering the information needed for the editorial process
process_profile.results:
• All information sources and contacts are identified and documented
• Information is automatically sent to the editorial team via push notifications
• Information gaps are identified, documented, and addressed with corrective measures
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Define and document information needs
• Identify and document appropriate sources
• Conduct and document information gathering
process_profile.work_products:
• Bibliography
• List of Information Requests

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Employees have the practical knowledge to independently gather information

questions.text: Do employees familiar with technical documentation have a general idea, based on experience, of where to find the necessary information, or are the information sources at least roughly defined?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The necessary information is known
• The contacts for the necessary information and the sources of that information are known
• The contacts are involved at an early stage
• There is a guideline for the necessary information and the associated sources of information
• Objectives and procedures for gathering the information have been established and documented
levels.benefit:
• Reduced reliance on individuals
• Less time spent on repetitive information searches
• Improved traceability through systematic documentation of sources
--> Initial stability and reduced workload

questions.text: Has it been determined what information is required for the technical documentation during the editorial process?
Are the appropriate contacts for the necessary information or information sources identified, and are they involved at an early stage?
Is there a guideline outlining the required information and its sources, and is it updated periodically?
Have the objectives and procedures for conducting information gathering been established and documented?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Information is shared regularly using the push method
• A systematic information-gathering process with defined roles and responsibilities is in place
levels.benefit:
• Implementing the push principle ensures greater transparency and traceability of information flows
• Reliable information quality and completeness
• More efficient editorial work due to less duplication of effort and fewer rounds of corrections
--> Reliable processes and quality

questions.text: Is information regularly gathered using a push model from the departments to the editorial team (e.g., from individual departments or for specific pieces of information)?
Has a systematic information-gathering process with defined roles and responsibilities been established?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Information is delivered to the editorial team using a push model
• Redundancies in the information are avoided
• The information flow is comprehensively modeled and defined
• The information flow is regularly reviewed
• The editorial team relies on a single source of truth when gathering information
levels.benefit:
• Reduced susceptibility to errors by eliminating manual handoffs
• Better coordination with other departments—redundant work is reduced across departments
• Improved on-time delivery, as the necessary information is available as planned
--> Integration brings efficiency and consistency

questions.text: Is all the necessary information being sent to the editorial department using the push method?
Has a comprehensive information flow been modeled and defined? Is it reviewed on a regular basis?
Are redundancies avoided, and can the editorial team rely on a single source of truth?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The information is automatically and standardizedly sent to the editorial department
• The information gathering process is continuously improved
• Opportunities for improvement in information gathering are identified, evaluated, and implemented based on current developments
levels.benefit:
• Efficiency gains through the elimination of time spent on searching and coordination
• Information that is always up to date
Competitive advantage through faster time-to-market and implementation of changes
--> Proactivity and automation ensure strategic advantages

questions.text: Is the information sent to the editorial department in an automated and standardized format?
Is the process of gathering information being continuously improved?
Are opportunities for improvement in information gathering identified, evaluated, and implemented based on current developments?

dimensions.name: Editorial Process - Internal Collaboration
dimensions.description: Collaboration among employees who work on or are responsible for technical documentation, both with one another and with other relevant departments

process_profile.purpose: Collaboration among employees who work on or are responsible for technical documentation, both with one another and with other relevant departments
process_profile.results:
• Collaboration within departments
• Cross-departmental collaboration
• Cross-departmental team culture
• Holistic view of editorial and development processes
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Regular coordination meetings within the editorial team and across departments
• Fostering a collaborative corporate culture through training or guidelines
• Using shared tools and platforms for coordination and documentation
process_profile.work_products:
• Rules and practices for collaboration
• Roles and responsibilities matrix
• Shared information platforms or tools

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • When necessary, we collaborate with other editors or those responsible for documentation on a project-by-project basis

questions.text: Is there collaboration with other editors or those responsible for documentation on a project-by-project basis as needed?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• We occasionally collaborate with other editors to exchange information and share experiences
• Collaboration helps prevent duplicate work by multiple people within the editorial team
• Responsibilities within the editorial team are defined and documented on a project-by-project basis
• Information and content are occasionally shared with other staff members within the editorial team
levels.benefit:
• Improved transparency through standardized communication procedures
• Reduced reliance on individual employees
• Initial relief through designated points of contact or established protocols
--> initial transparency and relief

questions.text: Do you occasionally collaborate with other editors to exchange information and share experiences?
Does collaboration occasionally prevent duplicate work by multiple people within the editorial team?
Are responsibilities within the editorial department defined and documented on a project-by-project basis?
Is information or content occasionally shared with other staff members within the editorial department?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• There is a defined process for cross-departmental collaboration throughout the project
• Cross-departmental teams are regularly formed to collaborate
• Employees are aware of the information produced by other departments, which helps prevent duplicate work across departments
• Decisions regarding the development and editorial processes are made within the teams
levels.benefit:
• Improved predictability of workloads across departmental boundaries
• Reduced errors through clear lines of responsibility
• Reduced redundancy through cross-departmental collaboration
--> Efficiency and reliability

questions.text: Is there a defined process for cross-departmental collaboration throughout the project?
Are cross-departmental teams regularly formed to work together?
Are employees aware of the information gathered by other departments, thereby helping to avoid duplicate work across departments?
Are decisions regarding the development and editorial processes made within the teams?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Roles are clearly defined within the teams and are reviewed on a regular basis
• Cross-functional teamwork is managed using key performance indicators
• There is a strong belief within the teams that better results are achieved by working together than working alone
• Teamwork is supported by various tools
levels.benefit:
• Significantly improved knowledge sharing
• Greater involvement of various departments leads to fewer media breaks
• Increased employee satisfaction through clear communication channels
--> Integration brings speed, better quality, and higher satisfaction

questions.text: Are roles defined within the teams, and are they reviewed on a regular basis?
Is cross-functional teamwork managed using key performance indicators?
Is there a shared belief within the teams that they can achieve better results together than they can on their own?
Is teamwork supported by tools?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Team collaboration and communication are continuously improved
• New opportunities for collaboration are continuously identified, evaluated, and implemented
levels.benefit:
• Sustainable efficiency gains through continuous improvement
• The organization fosters a culture of knowledge sharing and transparency
• Strong innovation capabilities through cross-functional, seamless collaboration
--> Optimization ensures innovative strength and a collaborative corporate culture

questions.text: Is teamwork and communication being continuously improved?
Are new opportunities for collaboration continuously identified, evaluated, and implemented?

dimensions.name: Editorial Process - External Collaboration
dimensions.description: The editorial team’s collaboration with external partners outside the organization. These may include service providers for technical documentation (e.g., translation service providers) or partners involved in the development process (e.g., suppliers)

process_profile.purpose: The editorial team’s collaboration with external partners outside the organization. These may include service providers for technical documentation (e.g., translation service providers) or partners involved in the development process (e.g., suppliers)
process_profile.results:
• Relevant external partners have been identified and engaged, and their roles have been defined
• Communication and coordination channels with external stakeholders have been defined
• Agreements have been documented
• External contributions have been integrated and are consistent with the overall documentation
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Clear definition of interfaces, responsibilities, and quality criteria
• Regular coordination sessions or status meetings with external partners
• Use of tools and platforms
process_profile.work_products:
• Contracts, agreements, or service level agreements (SLAs) with external partners
• Documented communication and interface guidelines for external collaboration
• Shared work platforms or data exchange formats

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Communication takes place through third parties (e.g., project managers)

questions.text: Can external communication be handled by third parties (e.g., project management) on a case-by-case basis when necessary?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• There are designated points of contact at external partner organizations for the editorial team
• External collaboration occurs occasionally on a project-by-project basis
• Agreements with external partners are occasionally documented
levels.benefit:
• Building trust in specific partnerships
• Reduced time spent on searching for and coordinating with partners thanks to designated contacts
--> Initial stability through established partners

questions.text: Are there designated points of contact at external partners for the editorial team, and are these documented?
Does the editorial team occasionally collaborate with external partners on a project-by-project basis?
Are agreements with external partners occasionally documented?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Collaboration with external partners is governed by contract
• Standardized interfaces and data exchange formats are in place with partners for information sharing and for integrating external content into technical documentation
• Roles, responsibilities, and communication channels with external partners are clearly defined and documented
levels.benefit:
• Higher quality and consistency of external services
• Lower risk thanks to clearly defined processes and contracts
• Improved traceability and documentation of the collaboration
--> Increased quality and predictability

questions.text: Is the editorial team’s collaboration with external partners governed by a contract?
Are there standardized interfaces and data exchange formats for partners to facilitate the exchange of information and the integration of external content into the technical documentation?
Have roles, responsibilities, and communication channels with external partners been defined and documented?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• External collaboration involves the use of shared tools

• Performance is regularly monitored and managed using jointly defined KPIs

levels.benefit:
• Streamlined processes through the digital integration of external partners

Stronger ties and long-term partnerships with external stakeholders

questions.text: Does the editorial team use shared tools when collaborating with external partners?
Are common (quality) goals pursued in external collaborations?
Are performance metrics regularly reviewed and managed based on jointly defined KPIs (e.g., on-time delivery, content approval lead times)?

levels.acceptance_criteria:
• External collaboration is continuously improved in cooperation with partners
• New opportunities for collaboration are identified, evaluated, and implemented
• We work together to continuously improve documentation (e.g., through innovation partnerships)
levels.benefit:
• Competitive advantage through stable, high-performing partner networks

Are we working together to continuously improve the documentation (e.g., through innovation partnerships)?

dimensions.name: Editorial process - Legal compliance
dimensions.description: Ensuring that the technical documentation meets all relevant statutory, normative and regulatory requirements. The goal is to avoid legal risks, reduce product liability and ensure that products can be placed on the market and obtain approval.

process_profile.purpose: Ensuring that the technical documentation meets all relevant statutory, normative and regulatory requirements. The goal is to avoid legal risks, reduce product liability and ensure that products can be placed on the market and obtain approval.
process_profile.results:
• Applicable laws, standards and guidelines for the technical documentation are identified and known
• The documentation meets the relevant legal and normative requirements
• Evidence of compliance with legal requirements is available
• Changes in regulatory frameworks are promptly integrated into the editorial process
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Systematic research of relevant standards, laws and guidelines for the documentation.
• Translation of legal requirements into concrete editorial guidelines and processes.
• Regular updates of the legal and standard situation in the editorial process.
• Performing compliance checks during and after document creation
process_profile.work_products:
• Documented legal and standard research (e.g. requirements lists, standards registers).
• Checklists or inspection documents to ensure compliance.
• Evidence of legal compliance (e.g. test reports, approval protocols, certificates).
• Versioned documentation states that make the timing of the legal review traceable

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Research into legal requirements is project-specific
• Employees individually possess knowledge of relevant standards
• Employees have individual standards libraries

questions.text: Can the research into legal requirements be carried out on a project or event-related basis when required?
Do employees individually have knowledge of the relevant standards? Do they have individual standards libraries?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Relevant guidelines and standards have been made generally accessible to the employees
• The research is carried out according to a defined process
• The conduct of the research is documented
• The currency of the guidelines and standards is checked occasionally
levels.benefit:
• Increased legal certainty through initial guidelines
--> initial security through occasional application

questions.text: Are relevant guidelines and standards made generally accessible to employees?
Is the research carried out according to a defined process?
Is the conduct of the research documented for the purpose of traceability and reuse?
Is the currency of the guidelines and standards occasionally reviewed?

levels.name: defined

• Documented processes or guidelines to ensure legal compliance in the documentation are in place
• The processes are an integral part of the editorial process
• Responsibilities for identifying and implementing legal requirements are assigned
• There are systematic audit procedures to ensure compliance with legal requirements
levels.benefit:
• Significantly reduced liability risk through systematic safeguards
• Reliability and consistency in implementing requirements
• Improved traceability in the event of an audit or inspection
--> reliable and efficient standardisation

questions.text: Are there documented processes or guidelines in place to ensure legal compliance in the documentation? Are the processes an integral part of the editorial process?
Are responsibilities for identifying and implementing legal requirements assigned?
Are there systematic audit procedures to ensure compliance with legal requirements?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:

questions.text: Are legal requirements regularly reviewed and their compliance actively monitored?

--> Proactive control becomes a strategic competitive advantage

Is the research into laws and standards conducted with AI support? Does AI assist in deriving application-specific measures to ensure legal compliance?

dimensions.name: Editorial process - Quality assurance
dimensions.description: Quality assurance in the editorial process serves to ensure the accuracy, completeness, comprehensibility and consistency of the technical documentation. The goal is to prevent errors, ensure user-friendliness and ensure compliance with internal and external standards.

process_profile.purpose: Quality assurance in the editorial process serves to ensure the accuracy, completeness, comprehensibility and consistency of the technical documentation. The goal is to prevent errors, ensure user-friendliness and ensure compliance with internal and external standards.
process_profile.results:
• The contents of the documentation are regularly reviewed and approved.
• Consistency, terminology and formal standards are complied with.
• Errors, inconsistencies or missing information are identified and corrected.
• Evidence of the inspections and approvals carried out is available.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Conduct formal and content checks (expert review, language review, standards review).
• Use of standardised methods and tools (e.g. style checkers).
• Defined procedures for approvals and correction cycles.
• Systematic recording and processing of errors or need for changes.
• Involvement of relevant specialist departments in review and approval processes.
process_profile.work_products:
• Review and approval documents (checklists, inspection protocols, approval forms).
• Guidelines and standards for documentation quality (e.g. style guides).
• Versioned documentation states with traceable history.
• Documented corrective actions and lessons learned.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• In the event of a deficit or damage case, quality assurance measures are carried out
• There are initial quality characteristics for assessing the editorial process

questions.text: Are quality assurance measures carried out at least situationally in the event of a deficit or damage case?
Do initial quality characteristics (e.g. adherence to deadlines) exist for assessing the editorial process?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Simple methods for quality assurance are carried out
• Quality assurance measures that have been carried out are occasionally documented
levels.benefit: • Greater reliability for recurring tasks

questions.text: Are simple methods of quality assurance (e.g. four-eyes principle, checklists) carried out?
Are quality assurance measures that have been carried out occasionally documented?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• There are documented processes and responsibilities for quality assurance of the documentation
• These processes are anchored in the editorial department
• Standardised audit procedures are used to ensure the quality of the documentation
• The release of the documentation is tied to defined quality criteria

dimensions.name: Knowledge management - Knowledge storage
dimensions.description: Knowledge storage describes the extent to which knowledge is documented, structured and stored sustainably in the organisation. The aim is to ensure permanent access to relevant knowledge and to reduce dependencies on individuals.

process_profile.purpose: Knowledge storage describes the extent to which knowledge is documented, structured and stored sustainably in the organisation. The aim is to ensure permanent access to relevant knowledge and to reduce dependencies on individuals.
process_profile.results:
• Knowledge is available for the documentation and recorded in a traceable manner.
• Dependencies on implicit knowledge of individual persons are reduced.
• Information sources are clearly identified and maintained.
• Knowledge is structured and accessible to various stakeholders.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Recording knowledge in a comprehensible form.
• Structuring and maintaining knowledge content.
• Use of IT systems for storage and provision.
• Regular updating and validation of knowledge assets.
• Ensuring access for relevant roles and departments.
process_profile.work_products:
• Knowledge documentation (e.g. manuals, internal guidelines, wikis).
• Protocols, lessons learned, best-practice collections.
• Systems for storage (e.g. knowledge databases, DMS, intranet).
• Structured formats for knowledge access (e.g. metadata, search functions).

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Knowledge in the organisation is bound to individuals
• Knowledge is predominantly passed on implicitly
• Knowledge is distributed in different places in the company

questions.text: Does relevant experiential and action knowledge exist in the organisation?
Is there an exchange of experiential and action knowledge within the organisation (e.g. orally, ad hoc)?
Are there storage locations in the company where knowledge is stored and made accessible (e.g. local files, emails)?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Initial storage structures with individual structure exist
• Implicit knowledge is occasionally documented
levels.benefit:
• initial transparency and accessibility beyond individual boundaries
• fewer losses of knowledge due to central storage

questions.text: Do initial storage structures (e.g. file folders, SharePoint) with individual structure exist?
Is implicit knowledge occasionally documented?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Knowledge is systematically documented and stored in fixed formats
• Organisationally regulated storage locations exist
• Knowledge is findable and traceable for the involved roles
levels.benefit:
• consistent structure and findability
• easier collaboration and knowledge exchange
• lower risk of knowledge loss

questions.text: Is knowledge systematically documented and stored in fixed formats?
Do organisationally regulated storage locations exist (e.g. central file storage, wiki)?
Is knowledge findable and traceable for the involved roles (access rights, versioning)?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Central systems are used for knowledge storage
• There are defined processes to update knowledge regularly
• Access to stored knowledge is guaranteed for all relevant stakeholders
• Storage is taxonomy-based and aimed at sustainable knowledge building
levels.benefit:
• higher efficiency due to rapid findability of information
• central, secure storage with clear responsibilities
• transparency about available knowledge and knowledge gaps
• better integration into business processes

questions.text: Are central systems (e.g. DMS, knowledge databases) used for knowledge storage?
Are there defined processes to update knowledge regularly?
Is access to stored knowledge guaranteed for all relevant stakeholders?
Is the storage taxonomy-based and aimed at sustainable knowledge building?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The organisation's knowledge is contained in semantic networks and linked with organisational systems
• Knowledge systems are continuously maintained and proactively expanded
• Innovative technologies support knowledge management
levels.benefit:
• maximum availability and currency of knowledge
• strategic advantage through the use of knowledge as a resource for innovation
• continuous optimisation of processes through data-based knowledge management
• high resilience through low dependency on individuals

questions.text: Is the organisation's knowledge contained in semantic networks (e.g. ontology)? Is it linked to organisational systems (e.g. ERP)?
Are knowledge systems continuously maintained and proactively expanded?
Do innovative technologies support knowledge management (e.g. AI-supported knowledge linking)?

dimensions.name: Knowledge management - Services
dimensions.description: The subdimension “Services” describes the extent to which the information available in the company and the associated knowledge are used as a service to create additional added value for internal departments or external customers. The aim is to strategically deploy knowledge from the company and in particular from the editorial or documentation department and to increase the value contribution of the documentation to the organisation.

process_profile.purpose: The subdimension “Services” describes the extent to which the information available in the company and the associated knowledge are used as a service to create additional added value for internal departments or external customers. The aim is to strategically deploy knowledge from the company and in particular from the editorial or documentation department and to increase the value contribution of the documentation to the organisation.
process_profile.results:
• Information from the documentation is specifically used for internal or external services.
• Documentation content supports service processes, e.g. training, support or knowledge databases.
• Services are structured and transparently embedded in the documentation process.
• Documentation is understood as a knowledge service that contributes to business success.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Using documented content for internal and external service offerings.
• Developing formats that make documentation accessible for services (e.g. FAQs, e-learning).
• Systematic collaboration between editorial, service and support.
• Embedding documentation content in digital service platforms.
• Continuous improvement of services based on feedback and usage data.
process_profile.work_products:
• Training materials, tutorials or training based on the documentation.
• Service or support portals that embed documentation content.
• Self-service databases or chatbots with integrated documentation information.
• Reports or evaluations on the use of documentation-based services.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • The documentation is used exclusively for legally or normatively required purposes and offers no added value otherwise

questions.text: Is the documentation used at least for legally or normatively required purposes (e.g. instruction) and does it otherwise offer no added value?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Initial informal assistance is offered
• Documentation is occasionally used for internal services
levels.benefit: • individual support of individual employees

questions.text: Are initial informal assistance (e.g. FAQs) offered?
Is documentation occasionally used for internal services (e.g. as a basis for training)?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The documentation is regularly used for service offerings
• Defined interfaces exist between documentation and service areas
• Structured coordination between editorial and service managers takes place
levels.benefit:
• plannable and consistent support
• reduced dependence on individual experts

questions.text: Is the documentation regularly used for service offerings (e.g. help desk, support portals)?
Do defined interfaces exist between documentation and service areas?
Is there structured coordination between editorial and service managers?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Knowledge is understood as a service
• Content is specifically prepared to optimally support services
• Services are regularly updated with documentation-based content
levels.benefit:
• scalable provision of knowledge for large user groups
• relief of experts through automated services
• constant availability of knowledge

questions.text: Is knowledge understood as a service (e.g. guided troubleshooting, interactive assistants, editorially maintained knowledge databases)?
Is content specifically prepared to optimally support services (e.g. modular FAQs, tutorials)?
Are services regularly updated with documentation-based content?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Proactive knowledge services with intelligent search, chatbots, context-related aids and integration into smart services are offered
• Continuous further development of documentation-supported services takes place based on feedback and usage analyses
• Documentation-based services are actively understood as a strategic added value for customers or internal departments
levels.benefit:
• maximum user-friendliness through personalised support
• strategic advantage through continuous improvement of processes
• promotion of innovations and organisational learning
• knowledge is actively used as a competitive advantage

questions.text: Are proactive knowledge services with intelligent search, chatbots, context-related aids and integration into smart services (e.g. AR, IoT, digital twins) offered?
Is continuous further development of documentation-supported services carried out based on feedback and usage analyses?
Are documentation-based services actively understood as a strategic added value for customers or internal departments?

dimensions.name: Organisational anchoring of technical documentation - Technical editorial team
dimensions.description: The subdimension 'Technical editorial team' describes the extent to which the creation, maintenance and further development of technical documentation is professionally organised and anchored in the organisation in terms of personnel. The aim is to establish clear responsibilities, ensure specialist expertise and guarantee the quality of the documentation independently of individual persons.

process_profile.purpose: The subdimension 'Technical editorial team' describes the extent to which the creation, maintenance and further development of technical documentation is professionally organised and anchored in the organisation in terms of personnel. The aim is to establish clear responsibilities, ensure specialist expertise and guarantee the quality of the documentation independently of individual persons.
process_profile.results:
• Clear responsibilities for technical documentation are defined.
• Documentation is no longer produced on a side-line basis but within a professional editorial framework.
• There is an organisational unit or function for the technical editorial team.
• The expertise of the editors is secured and continuously developed.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Definition of clear responsibilities for the creation and maintenance of documentation.
• Establishment of an editorial function or department.
• Ensuring specialist and methodological qualifications of the editors.
• Integration of the editorial team into company processes (e.g. development, service, quality).
• Continuous professionalisation and further development of the editorial team.
process_profile.work_products:
• Role and task descriptions for the technical editorial team.
• Organisational charts or responsibility assignments.
• Qualification profiles or training certificates for editors.
• Editorial guidelines or manuals.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Documentation is created in passing by specialist departments or individual persons without editorial responsibility
• The required skills are acquired independently out of necessity

questions.text: Is documentation at least prepared by specialist departments or individuals without formally defined editorial responsibility?
Are the skills required for documentation at least acquired independently out of practical necessity?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Individual persons are named who, alongside other tasks, are responsible for documentation
• Documentation is carried out in occasionally repeatable processes without defined editorial structures
• Employees who have previously taken on editorial tasks are named and subsequently trained in the required areas
levels.benefit:
• Relief for specialist departments
• initial specialisation and build-up of expertise

questions.text: Are at least individual persons named who (in addition to other tasks) are responsible for documentation?
Is documentation carried out at least occasionally in repeatable processes, even if no defined editorial structures are in place?
Are employees who take on editorial tasks at least partially named and given further training in the required areas as needed?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• There is a clearly defined editorial role or a small editorial unit and a competence profile exists
• Responsibilities and processes for documentation are bindingly regulated
• The persons involved have basic qualifications in technical documentation
levels.benefit:
• Consistent, legally compliant and user-friendly documentation
• Clear responsibilities for content
• Better coordination with specialist departments
• Professional standard in content

questions.text: Is there a clearly defined editorial role or small editorial unit? Does a defined competence profile exist?
Are responsibilities and processes for documentation bindingly regulated?
Do the persons involved have basic qualifications in technical documentation?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The editorial team is established organisationally as a permanent function or department
• The technical editorial team is integrated into central company processes
• The technical editorial team has an enterprise-wide mindset regarding the management of data and content
• Targeted further training and professionalisation of the editors takes place
levels.benefit:
• Efficiency gains through processes and tools
• Scalability with increasing documentation requirements
• Systematic quality assurance
• Close integration with product development and service

questions.text: Is the editorial team established organisationally as a permanent function or department?
Is the technical editorial team integrated into central company processes (e.g. development, quality assurance, service)?
Does the technical editorial team have an enterprise-wide mindset for managing data and content (content strategists)?

Is there targeted further training and professionalisation of the editors away from pure writing towards content engineering (e.g. maintenance of metadata models, development and updating of editorial rules)?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The technical editorial team is anchored as a strategic function and actively contributes to the company’s success
• The editorial team is a central partner in interdisciplinary projects
• Technical editors continually expand their role towards semantic modellers
levels.benefit:
• Documentation becomes an integral part of products and services
• Competitive advantage through high-quality and innovative content strategies
• Strengthening customer satisfaction and market differentiation

questions.text: Is the technical editorial team anchored as a strategic function and does it actively contribute to the success of the company?
Is the editorial team a central partner in interdisciplinary projects (e.g. digitalisation, Industry 4.0, knowledge management)?
Do the technical editors continuously expand their role towards semantic modellers?

dimensions.name: Organisational anchoring of technical documentation - Importance of technical documentation
dimensions.description: The subdimension 'Importance of technical documentation' describes the extent to which technical documentation is understood and promoted within the company as a significant component of products and services. The aim is to evolve documentation from a mere obligatory task into a recognised, strategically relevant factor for success.

process_profile.purpose: The subdimension 'Importance of technical documentation' describes the extent to which technical documentation is understood and promoted within the company as a significant component of products and services. The aim is to evolve documentation from a mere obligatory task into a recognised, strategically relevant factor for success.
process_profile.results:
• The importance of documentation is clearly defined and anchored in the company.
• Documentation is no longer understood exclusively as a regulatory obligation.
• Documentation demonstrably contributes to value creation, customer satisfaction and differentiation.
• Documentation is part of the corporate strategy and is actively promoted.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Recognition of documentation beyond regulatory minimum requirements.
• Communication of the benefits of documentation within the organisation.
• Allocation of resources in proportion to the importance of documentation.
• Integration of documentation into value creation and service processes.
• Strategic positioning of documentation as part of corporate success.
process_profile.work_products:
• Company policies or mission statements that record the importance of documentation.
• Budget and resource decisions that reflect the value of documentation.
• Communication measures that make the benefits of documentation visible.
• Key performance indicators (KPIs) that demonstrate the value contribution of documentation (e.g. service effort, customer satisfaction).

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Documentation is created solely to meet legal or normative requirements

questions.text: Is at least the relevance of documentation recognised for fulfilling legal or normative requirements within the organisation?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Documentation is occasionally perceived as helpful for internal processes or customers
• There are initial approaches to using documentation beyond duty
levels.benefit: • initial recognition of the importance for product usage

questions.text: Is documentation at least occasionally perceived as helpful for internal processes or customers?
Are there initial approaches to using documentation beyond the obligatory requirement (e.g. as an internal source of knowledge)?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Documentation is regularly used in service, support or training processes
• The benefit of documentation for efficiency and customer satisfaction is recognised in the organisation
• Documentation is regarded as part of product quality
• There are initial attempts to make the quality or contribution of documentation measurable
levels.benefit: • Clear role of documentation in the product life cycle

questions.text: Is documentation used regularly in service, support or training processes?
Is the benefit of documentation for efficiency and customer satisfaction recognised in the organisation? Is documentation regarded as part of product quality?
Are there initial attempts to make the quality or contribution of documentation measurable?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• A defined, holistic content strategy exists in the organisation
• Documentation is anchored organisationally and in terms of budget so that its contribution to the company’s success is assured
• Documentation is part of quality, compliance and product strategies
• The value of documentation is communicated regularly and supported by key figures
levels.benefit:
• Contribution to competitiveness through professional user support
• Internal relief through structured processes and reuse

questions.text: Does a defined, holistic content strategy exist in the organisation?
Is documentation organisationally and in terms of budget anchored such that its contribution to the company's success is assured?
Is documentation part of quality, compliance and product strategies?
Is the value of documentation communicated regularly and supported by key figures?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Technical documentation is anchored as a strategic factor in corporate strategy
• Continuous monitoring makes the importance of documentation visible via KPIs
• Documentation is deliberately used as a differentiating feature in competition and as an enabler of new business models
levels.benefit:
• Significant competitive advantage through utilisation of technical documentation
• Strategic use for digital services and customer retention
• Strengthening of the corporate brand through excellent user communication
• Documentation as a driver of innovation

questions.text: Is technical documentation anchored as a strategic factor in corporate strategy?
Is there continuous monitoring that makes the importance of documentation visible through KPIs?
Is it deliberately used as a differentiating feature in competition (e.g. faster time to market) and as an enabler of new business models?

dimensions.name: Organisational anchoring of technical documentation - Cultural anchoring
dimensions.description: The subdimension 'Cultural anchoring' describes the extent to which technical documentation is anchored in the corporate culture and is perceived by employees and managers as a self-evident part of products, processes and customer experiences. The aim is to establish documentation not just as an obligation but as an integral part of the quality mindset and innovation culture.

process_profile.purpose: The subdimension 'Cultural anchoring' describes the extent to which technical documentation is anchored in the corporate culture and is perceived by employees and managers as a self-evident part of products, processes and customer experiences. The aim is to establish documentation not just as an obligation but as an integral part of the quality mindset and innovation culture.
process_profile.results:
• Documentation is recognised as important and self-evident by all relevant stakeholders.
• Documentation is part of the organisation's understanding of quality and service.
• Employees actively contribute to providing and maintaining documentation-relevant information.
• Documentation is culturally embedded in decision-making processes and projects.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Communication of the importance of documentation by management.
• Anchoring of documentation in the company's values or guidelines.
• Promotion of a culture in which knowledge and information are shared.
• Motivation of employees to actively support documentation.
• Recognition of contributions to documentation as part of performance and success.
process_profile.work_products:
• Mission statements, company values or communication guidelines that include documentation.
• Training materials and awareness materials on the importance of documentation.
• Internal communication campaigns (e.g. intranet, newsletters) that highlight the importance of documentation.
• Success stories or best practices that make the value of documented content visible.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Awareness of the importance of documentation exists only among the employees who work with documentation
• Isolated opinions are expressed that long-term process improvement is more important than short-term project budget adherence

questions.text: Is there at least awareness of the importance of documentation among the employees who work with documentation?
Are there isolated opinions that long-term process improvement is more important than short-term project budget adherence?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Initial approaches to internally communicating the importance of documentation exist
• There is sporadic support from managers or departments for technical documentation
• Documentation is highlighted as important by individual managers or departments
levels.benefit: • Increasing awareness of the value of documentation

questions.text: Are there initial approaches to communicating the importance of documentation internally?
Is there sporadic support from managers or departments for technical documentation?
Is documentation highlighted as important by individual managers or departments?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Documentation meets with broad acceptance in the company
• Recurring communication measures emphasise the importance of documentation and this is recognised within the organisation
levels.benefit:
• Promotion of collaboration and knowledge sharing
• Increasing acceptance leads to better quality

questions.text: Does documentation meet with broad acceptance in the company?
Are there recurring communication measures that emphasise the importance of documentation? Is this recognised in the organisation?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Documentation is an integral part of the organisational culture and anchored in values, mission statements or quality guidelines
• The overall process is in focus even after completion of an individual project
• The organisational culture promotes active knowledge sharing and support of documentation
levels.benefit:
• Efficiency gains in projects through a shared knowledge base
• Better collaboration between departments
• Promotion of a sense of responsibility for content

questions.text: Is documentation an integral part of the organisational culture? Is it anchored in the organisation's values, mission statements or quality guidelines?
Is the overall process in focus even after completion of an individual project (process management perspective)?
Does the organisational culture promote active knowledge sharing and support of documentation?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• The documentation culture is exemplary, lived internally and communicated externally
• Technical documentation is a natural part of the organisation's overall quality and innovation mindset
levels.benefit:
• Continuous quality improvement through broad acceptance
• Contribution to the capacity for innovation through shared knowledge
• Sustainable anchoring of documentation as a competitive advantage
• Identification of employees and customers with the company's values

questions.text: Is the documentation culture exemplary, lived internally and communicated externally (e.g. in CSR, recruitment)?
Is technical documentation a natural part of the organisation's overall quality and innovation mindset?

dimensions.name: Interfaces - Internal interfaces
dimensions.description: 'Internal interfaces' describes the design, use and further development of handovers between departments, roles and IT systems within an organisation. The goal is to make the flow of information consistent, efficient and error-free in order to avoid duplicate work, information losses and media disruptions and to ensure an integrated way of working.

process_profile.purpose: 'Internal interfaces' describes the design, use and further development of handovers between departments, roles and IT systems within an organisation. The goal is to make the flow of information consistent, efficient and error-free in order to avoid duplicate work, information losses and media disruptions and to ensure an integrated way of working.
process_profile.results:
• Smooth, traceable and standardised handovers within the organisation.
• Clear responsibilities and accountabilities at interfaces.
• Uniform data formats and processes for internal information flows.
• Transparency regarding interface dependencies and their quality.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Identification and prioritisation of relevant interfaces within the organisation.
• Definition of handover procedures and standards (e.g. file formats, data structures).
• Establishment of responsibilities for interface maintenance.
• Regular monitoring and optimisation of interface quality.
• Introduction of technical integrations (e.g. use of APIs, middleware).
process_profile.work_products:
• Interface descriptions (e.g. between ERP, CMS, PDM, MES).
• Process documentation with clearly defined handover points.
• Data mappings and integration guidelines.
• Monitoring and error logs for interfaces.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Information exchange between departments takes place predominantly informally

questions.text: Does the exchange of information between departments take place predominantly informally (e.g. by e-mail, telephone, Excel)?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Initial handovers between certain departments or systems are clearly identified
• Initial standards or formats for internal handovers exist but are not applied consistently
• Responsibilities have already been defined for some interfaces
levels.benefit:
• Improved orientation
• Reduced information losses

questions.text: Are initial handovers between certain departments or systems clearly identified (e.g. ERP → editorial)?
Do initial standards or formats for internal handovers exist, even if they are not applied consistently?
Are responsibilities already defined for some interfaces?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Internal interface processes are documented and transparent for all participants
• Defined standards and formats are applied consistently
• Roles and responsibilities for interfaces are clearly regulated
• Training, guidelines or tools support the use of the interfaces
levels.benefit:
• Reliable and efficient collaboration
• Clear responsibilities and procedures
• Reduced coordination efforts
• Higher quality and consistency of content

questions.text: Are internal interface processes documented and transparent for all participants?
Are defined standards and formats (e.g. file formats, data structures) applied consistently?
Are roles and responsibilities for interfaces clearly regulated?
Do training, guidelines or tools support the use of the interfaces?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Central systems are technically integrated so that handovers are automated
• Information is consistently available to several departments, almost in real time
• The quality of the interfaces is monitored regularly
• Errors that occur are documented and systematically rectified
levels.benefit:
• Automation reduces manual errors and coordination requirements
• Faster information flows
• Increased transparency and traceability

questions.text: Are central systems (e.g. ERP, CMS, PDM) technically integrated so that handovers are automated?
Is information consistently available to several departments (almost) in real time?
Is the quality of interfaces monitored regularly (e.g. through monitoring or error messages)?
Are errors that occur documented and systematically rectified?

levels.name: optimized
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• All relevant systems are fully integrated via a continuous architecture
• Adjustments and optimisations are carried out proactively to cover future organisational or technological requirements at an early stage
• Data flows are fully automated, in real time and without redundancies
levels.benefit:
• Efficiency through cross-departmental collaboration
• Promotion of innovation through continuous knowledge exchange
• Documentation becomes the connecting element between departments

questions.text: Are all relevant systems fully integrated via a continuous architecture (e.g. APIs, middleware)?
Are adjustments and optimisations carried out proactively in order to cover future organisational or technological requirements at an early stage?
Are data flows fully automated, in real time and without redundancies?

dimensions.name: Interfaces - External interfaces
dimensions.description: 'External interfaces' describes the definition, use and further development of handovers and data exchanges between your organisation and external partners, customers or suppliers. The goal is to make the flow of information across organisational boundaries secure, standardised and interoperable.

process_profile.purpose: 'External interfaces' describes the definition, use and further development of handovers and data exchanges between your organisation and external partners, customers or suppliers. The goal is to make the flow of information across organisational boundaries secure, standardised and interoperable.
process_profile.results:
• Clear, traceable interfaces with external stakeholders.
• Secure and standardised data flows.
• Integration into external systems and industry-specific standards.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Analysis of external requirements.
• Implementation of exchange formats and standards.
• Securing data exchange (e.g. encryption, access rights).
• Regular quality assurance of external interfaces.
process_profile.work_products:
• Interface descriptions for external partners (e.g. customer portals, supplier databases).
• Defined exchange formats (e.g. XML, JSON, iiRDS, ECLASS).
• Agreements on security and approvals.
• Monitoring reports on external data transfers.

levels.name: initial

• Reduction of misunderstandings and duplicate work

Are external interface processes integrated into internal procedures and regularly usable?

levels.acceptance_criteria:
• External interfaces are technically integrated so that data is exchanged automatically

• The quality of the interfaces is regularly monitored and documented

• Faster information flows and less manual effort

dimensions.name: Technological infrastructure - Editorial IT infrastructure
dimensions.description: The 'Editorial IT infrastructure' describes the extent to which the IT systems and tools required for technical documentation are available, integrated and usable. The aim is to support documentation efficiently, consistently, collaboratively and without media discontinuity.

process_profile.purpose: The 'Editorial IT infrastructure' describes the extent to which the IT systems and tools required for technical documentation are available, integrated and usable. The aim is to support documentation efficiently, consistently, collaboratively and without media discontinuity.
process_profile.results:
• The IT infrastructure used supports the creation, management and delivery of technical documentation.
• Systems are adapted to editorial requirements and integrated into work processes.
• The infrastructure enables efficient collaboration, reuse and quality assurance.
• The IT infrastructure is scalable, future-oriented and supports the level of digitisation of the documentation.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Use of appropriate tools for creating and managing documentation.
• Customisation and configuration of the systems to editorial requirements.
• Establishment of interfaces to adjacent company systems.
• Ensuring system availability, data integrity and IT support.
• Continuous further development of the infrastructure in line with new requirements (e.g. modular content, omnichannel publication).
process_profile.work_products:
• System landscape (e.g. word processing, editorial systems, CCMS, terminology databases, translation tools).
• IT policies and integration concepts for editorial systems.
• Interface descriptions to adjacent systems (e.g. PLM, ERP, PIM).
• Documentation of processes and workflows supported by the infrastructure.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Tools that are ad hoc most useful and accessible are used

questions.text: Are at least tools used that are ad hoc most useful and accessible (e.g. Word)?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Tools with dedicated layout support are used
• Initial specialised tools such as terminology databases or translation software are used in addition
• The use of these tools takes place on a case-by-case basis and without systematic integration
levels.benefit:
• Higher quality of individual work steps
• Relief in routine tasks
→ initial quality gains through specialised tools

questions.text: Are tools with dedicated layout support (e.g. Word add-ins) used?

dimensions.name: Technological infrastructure - Organisational IT infrastructure
dimensions.description: The 'Organisational IT infrastructure' describes the maturity of a company's general IT landscape. The aim is to provide a stable, secure and high-performance infrastructure that efficiently supports collaboration, data management and business processes and can flexibly cover future requirements.

process_profile.purpose: The 'Organisational IT infrastructure' describes the maturity of a company's general IT landscape. The aim is to provide a stable, secure and high-performance infrastructure that efficiently supports collaboration, data management and business processes and can flexibly cover future requirements.
process_profile.results:
• The IT infrastructure is stable, secure and supports business processes reliably.
• Collaboration and information exchange within the organisation are effectively enabled.
• Data and systems are protected, consistent and available.
• The infrastructure is scalable and is regularly adapted to new requirements.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Operation of basic IT systems to support core processes.
• Provision of platforms for communication and collaboration.
• Implementation of IT security measures and data protection concepts.
• Integration of the IT infrastructure into the organisation's overall strategy.
• Continuous adaptation and modernisation of the infrastructure.
process_profile.work_products:
• Overview of the IT architecture (network, servers, storage, cloud services).
• IT security policies and access rules.
• Collaboration and communication platforms (e.g. intranet, project management tools).
• Documentation of IT support, availability and failure management.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Master data exists in various systems
• Only basic IT facilities without central coordination are available

questions.text: Is master data present in various systems?
Are at least basic IT facilities (PCs, e-mail, office) available without central coordination?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Company systems are used for individual applications
• Initial central IT systems such as file servers, intranet or standard collaboration tools exist
• Basic IT security measures are implemented
levels.benefit:
• Better collaboration through shared tools
• Reduced duplication of work
• Improved transparency in individual processes

questions.text: Are company systems used for individual applications (e.g. for material management)?
Are there initial central IT systems such as file servers, intranet or standard collaboration tools?
Are basic IT security measures implemented?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Systems such as ERP or PLM systems are institutionally anchored
• The IT infrastructure is stable and reliably available for essential business processes
• The infrastructure systematically supports data exchange and collaboration within the organisation
• Roles, access rights and responsibilities are clearly defined
levels.benefit:
• Higher efficiency and reliability
• Clear responsibilities and support structures
• Lower IT costs through standardisation
• Simplified onboarding of new employees

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• An integrated system landscape with data reconciliation and exchange across system boundaries exists

• The infrastructure is actively maintained and optimised
levels.benefit:
• Efficiency gains through continuous data flows
• Fewer errors due to automated interfaces
• Better basis for decision-making through consistent data
• Simplified collaboration between departments
