dimensions.name: Technological infrastructure - Digitalisation of products
dimensions.description: The 'Digitalisation of products' describes the extent to which products support digital functions, data connectivity and networking. The aim is to increase the value of products through digital enhancements, services and integrations and to embed them in modern digital ecosystems.

process_profile.purpose: The 'Digitalisation of products' describes the extent to which products support digital functions, data connectivity and networking. The aim is to increase the value of products through digital enhancements, services and integrations and to embed them in modern digital ecosystems.
process_profile.results:
• Depending on the maturity level, products are digitalised to varying degrees – from purely mechanical/analogue to fully networked and software-based.
• Digital functions create added value for customers and users.
• Data generated by products can be leveraged for services, maintenance and improvements.
• Products are networked and integrated into digital platforms.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Analysis of the current degree of digitalisation in the product portfolio.
• Integration of digital functions into products (e.g. sensors, software modules).
• Development of interfaces for networking with other systems (e.g. fieldbuses, cloud platforms).
• Structured collection and use of product data.
• Continuous further development of digital product strategies and roadmaps.
process_profile.work_products:
• Documentation of product functions (analogue, digital, hybrid).
• Concepts and specifications for interfaces (e.g. APIs, fieldbuses, cloud connectivity).
• Roadmaps and strategies for product digitalisation.
• Customer or market feedback on the acceptance of digital product functions.

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Products consist predominantly of mechanical or analogue components without digital functions

questions.text: Do products consist at least of mechanical or analogue components without digital functions?

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Products have individual digital components
• These functions are largely isolated and not networked
levels.benefit:
• Initial additional functions for customers
• Emerging differentiation in the market

questions.text: Do products partially have individual digital components (e.g. displays, simple sensors)?
Are these functions largely isolated and not networked?

levels.name: defined
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Products contain standardised interfaces that allow connection to other systems
• Product data can be collected and passed on in a structured manner
levels.benefit:
• Increased product quality and functionality
• Reduced service and maintenance costs
• Better data basis for internal processes

questions.text: Do products contain standardised interfaces (e.g. Ethernet, fieldbus, API) that allow connection to other systems?
Can product data be collected and passed on in a structured way?

levels.name: quantitatively managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Products are capable of capturing and processing data in a context-sensitive manner
• Digital services or functions are controlled by metadata or software
• Integration into digital platforms has been implemented
levels.benefit:
• New business models
• Real-time monitoring and proactive maintenance
• Closer customer loyalty through digital services

questions.text: Are products able to capture and process data in a context-sensitive manner (e.g. condition monitoring, remote monitoring)?
Are digital services or functions controlled by metadata or software?

levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Products are fully integrated into digital ecosystems
• Products support data-driven business models and enable new services
• Continuous data exchange with other products or manufacturers takes place
levels.benefit:
• Strategic competitive advantage through data-driven innovations
• Customer orientation through context-sensitive services
• New value chains and platform models
• High future viability through continuous further development

dimensions.name: Technological infrastructure - Production IT infrastructure
dimensions.description: The 'Production IT infrastructure' describes the maturity level of the IT systems used in production. The aim is to make production processes efficient, transparent, flexible and integrable through digital solutions and thus provide a foundation for Industry 4.0 and data-driven value creation.

process_profile.purpose: The 'Production IT infrastructure' describes the maturity level of the IT systems used in production. The aim is to make production processes efficient, transparent, flexible and integrable through digital solutions and thus provide a foundation for Industry 4.0 and data-driven value creation.
process_profile.results:
• Production processes are supported by IT and increasingly automated.
• Data from production is available for monitoring, analysis and optimisation.
• Systems are interconnected with each other and with adjacent areas (e.g. ERP, PLM).
• Production IT is secure, scalable and future-proof.
process_profile.basic_practices:
• Use of IT to support production processes.
• Implementation of monitoring and reporting functions.
• Integration of production systems with adjacent company systems.
• Ensuring IT security and stability in production.
• Further development of production IT with regard to automation, data integration and Industry 4.0.
process_profile.work_products:
• Documentation of production systems (e.g. MES, SCADA, IoT platforms).
• Integration concepts and interface descriptions (e.g. to ERP, quality management).
• Protocols and reports on production data (e.g. OEE, condition data, process parameters).
• Strategies for the further development of production IT (e.g. cloud, edge computing).

levels.name: initial
levels.acceptance_criteria: • Production is largely manually controlled, with only sporadic use of IT

questions.text: Is at least the use of simple IT solutions for controlling production planned? (e.g. simple Excel spreadsheets for manual operational data collection)

levels.name: managed
levels.acceptance_criteria:
• Basic IT solutions such as machine controllers are available
• Data is collected sporadically but not yet systematically and used

levels.benefit:

• Improved planning and control of production
• Reduced downtimes through structured workflows
• Lower operating costs through standardisation

• IT supports automated workflows and optimised production planning
• Continuous monitoring of production in real time takes place
levels.benefit:
• Real-time transparency of production data
• Better resource utilisation and planning accuracy
• Close interlinking between development, planning and production
